# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I've seen hundreds of comedies. Suggest me the movie that will make me laugh so hard that I will forget I have a 9-5 job

**Score:** 440
**Body:** I've seen most of the obvious ones, and a lot of the "funny but actually just clever" movies. I'm not looking for something that's merely witty, charming, or critically acclaimed. I just want to laugh even if it is stupid. I want my neighbor to complain about my laughing. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Galaxy Quest  
Dodgeball 
  - If you can dodge a wrench, you can dodge a ball!
2. Tucker and Dale Vs Evil
  - *We got yer friend*
3. History of the World Part 1

Airplane!

Death at a Funeral (UK)

Best in Show

This is Spinal Tap


4. Office Space!
5. There's Something About Mary
6. Wet hot American summer 
7. Rat race (2001)

Hot fuzz

Tucker and dale vs evil

We're the Millers (2013)

The other guys

Spy (2015)

Get smart (2008)

The Man who knew too litte (1997)

EDIT:  
HOT TUB TIME MACHINE

tropic thunder

Frequently Asked Questions About Time Travel

Kungfu Hustle
8. The hardest I have ever laughed at a movie was during the hotel fight in "Borat." I started getting tunnel vision from being unable to breathe.

## 2. Everyone is a piece of shit.

**Score:** 55
**Body:** Movies where everyone is a piece of shit. Doesn’t matter if it’s foreign movies or not. I’m looking for some movies where everyone- or the main character- is a piece of shit.

Let’s work it out so no confusions;

Main character: \[insert movie\]

Everyone in general: \[insert movie\]

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Saltburn. Almost everyone sucks.
2. The Wolf of Wall Street 👍 
3. Burn After Reading is an absolutely incredible one. Also Killer Joe.
4. Everyone in general: Very Bad Things (1998)
5. Safdie films.
6. Gone Girl. Nick, Amy, Margot, Desi, Lawyer, journalists, everyone. 
7. Not a movie,  but check out the TV series 'It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia"

Everyone is a piece of shit.
  - Succession too
8. Triangle of Sadness. Everyone in general (excluding Woody Harrelson)
9. Burn After Reading, Tar, Clockwork Orange, 

## 3. I want to invite a girl on a date outdoors with a projector. Can you recommend a romantic movie she might like?

**Score:** 23
**Body:** 'm planning a casual outdoor date with a projector, some blankets, and snacks. I'd like to pick a romantic movie that's sweet, fun, and enjoyable even if you're not usually into romance. Nothing too cheesy or overly dramatic. We're both in our 20s and still getting to know each other, so I'd like something with a good balance of romance, humor, and a great story.

What movies would you recommend for this kind of date night? What has worked well for you? 😊



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Amelie would be perfect for this
2. The Princess Bride
3. Ever After. Die beste Cinderella-Version, die ich kenne
4. Roman Holiday - a classic
5. Princess Bride 
6. My first date with my wife was Sam Raimi's Drag Me To Hell.  I bring it up every anniversary.
7. Go with About Time, it's sweet without being cringe and the whole father-son bit adds a nice layer that isn't just romance.
8. Depends on your date
9. Notting Hill
10. About Time is amazing and romantic

## 4. Movies where a character signs up for a mysterious service from a shady corporation and goes down a nightmarish rabbit hole

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Some examples of this include "Videodrome", "The Substance", and "The Game" by David Fincher. Also the "Quitters, Inc." segment of "Cat's Eye" if anyone's seen that. Usually about an individual who feels unfulfilled in life and discovers an evil corporation who offers them a mysterious service that turns their life upside-down, typically illuminating all of the main character's flaws and dark secrets in the process.

Some examples that don't quite fit the criteria in the title but still scratch the same itch are "Lost Highway", "Brain Dead" (the one with Pullman and Paxton, not the Peter Jackson one), and "Sorry to Bother You". I think the most important part to me is getting to see the layers slowly pulled back on a character's dull, humdrum life, revealing a dark conspiracy that submerges them in a kakfa-esque nightmare that changes them forever.

Kinda specific but hopefully some of you guys can recommend me some movies that at least sort of fit this criteria. Thanks

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind

Total Recall (lots of PKD fiction fits this template, but not on film)

Her (not that evil)
2. Not a movie but, The Chair Company
  - First thing that came to my mind, as well.
3. Devs (TV show tho not a movie)   
Severance (also a TV show that is basically movie quality)  
Being John Malkovich (less emphasis on the corporate angle than you may be looking for, but still involves a rabbit hole) 
4. mickey 17
5. Not a movie but there are episodes of black mirror that are like this. The episode with Rashida jones specifically
6. The Menu (2022). Charlie and the Chocolate Factory LOL. Moon (2009). Outland (1981). The Firm (1993) I think counts. The Innocents (1961) These last four are all 'person takes a job and it gets weird' Which is kind of like 'signing up' I think.
7. The Devil's Advocate (1997)
8. Death Becomes Her 
9. Total Recall

## 5. Suggest me a movie that you thought was really weird but also surprisingly good.

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I am looking for movies that are out of the ordinary; that shocked you by how unconventional they are, but you left thinking, that was actually a good movie despite all the weirdness.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Lobster 
2. The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent,  Swiss Army Man
3. Being John Malkovitch
4. Sorry to Bother You (2018)
5. Under the Skin (2013)
6. Videodrome.   Might rewatch it soon.
7. Vivarium 
8. Swiss Army Man
9. Phantom of the Paradise

Daisies 

Sorry to Bother You + I Love Boosters
10. The Ballad of Buster Scruggs (2018)

## 3. What are your picks for the most terrifying scenarios in movies?

**Score:** 613
**Body:** My personal pick is the fall of humanity in children of men 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Threads, The Day after, Contagion
  - Contagion is one of those movies I can rewatch again and again because of how good it is. Just watched it again four days ago. 
2. The Road shook me up
  - The book is worse.
3. 28 Days Later — fast zombies and the collapse of society, a terrifying combo
  - I feel like they were related.
  - Fucking legendary movie. 

It's easy to overlook now in the same way The Matrix and The Blair Witch Project are easy to overlook. They were all revolutionary ideas that have been iterated on so many times that they can never have the same meaning to a new generation because you genuinely just had to be there (and that's okay, that's just how shit works).
4. The Road (2009)

Threads (1984)
5. The scenario in gattaca.
6. Fun fact this movie canonically takes place next year.

## 5. Rate these two actors and their performances

**Score:** 323
**Body:** Jake Gyllenhaal’s the better actor on pure craft—sharper range, bolder choices, more willing to shatter his image. Ryan Gosling owns the screen through untouchable charisma and effortless cool. But stack their filmographies head to head, weigh the performances that actually matter, and you hit perfect parity. Different weapons, matched skill. I’d say It’s a tie.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I mean depends what you want out your movie! Jake can go super intense with a role (Ala his top 3) where as Ryan has a chill cool relatable guy
  - This right here. Love both of them but it depends largely on the vibe 
2. Nighcrawler imho is a 9
  - His acting was Crazy in that  movie 
  - There's a few movies in that list that should have a higher rating. 

Drive, Nice Guys, Prisoners, Zodiac are all better than those ratings imo. 
3. To not put place beyond the pines on here is wild 
4. ![gif](giphy|e0weElNxQfpt92S71M)
5. Ryan Gosling is great and I've loved every movie he's picked to be in, but Jake Gyllenhaal has an insane amount of range. He was a warm, empathetic guy in Brothers and a completely detached sociopath in Nightcrawler and they're both entirely believable
6. Having Gyllenhaal in Brokeback Mtn next to Gosling in Remember the Titans is hysterical
7. Is there a reason we don’t get goslings best acted roles and instead get shit like remember the titans?

No half Nelson, Barbie, blue valentine, place beyond the pines?

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Infernal Affairs: A Far More Subtle Cat-and-Mouse Thriller in Comparison to The Departed

**Score:** 57
**Body:** Got around to watching *Infernal Affairs* after hearing about it for some time. Knowing it was the film that inspired Scorsese's *The Departed*, I was interested to see how it tackled the same narrative and premise.

After watching it, I have to say that while I still love *The Departed,* I do think *Infernal Affairs* is the superior version in terms of its approach to the cat-and-mouse conflict. Whereas *The Departed* established a clear cut hero and villain with Billy Costigan and Colin Sullivan, *Infernal Affairs* is less black and white, with Inspector Lau Kin Ming and Chen Wing Yan each having their own flaws and morals. In fact, Lau, despite being the gangster turned inspector, is humanized far more than Colin as he does find himself growing attached to the police. The complexity of Lau and Chen also succeeds thanks to the two great performances by Andy Lau and Tony Leung who are equally phenomenal with the subtly they give to their characters.

I also think what makes the cat-and-mouse tale in *Infernal Affairs* more successful than *The Departed* is its subtle direction and style. The directors Wai Keung Lau and Alan Mak allow the narrative to breathe far more, relying less on heavy violence and more on character interactions while still having a dynamic style full of quick cuts, slow-mo, and rapid editing. The use of a light color palette also gives *Infernal Affairs* a very lo-fi aesthetic that gels well with the eye-catching cinematography. It's cool to see Christopher Doyle serve as the visual consultant here being familiar with his work with Wong Kar-Wai.

Overall, I definitely enjoyed *Infernal Affairs*. Whereas *The Departed* focused far more on engaging the viewer with violence and grit, *Infernal Affairs* is all about the little moments. It's unfair to say whether one is better given they're vastly different in tackling the cat-and-mouse conflict, but I can say *Infernal Affairs* deserves more credit given it's rarely talked about alongside *The Departed*.

  


### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I might be the only HK cop thriller fan who prefers The Departed. For me Infernal Affair is excellent but also has a generic vibe that the best of the genre don’t have. But it’s just preference. 
2. Okay. I still haven’t seen Infernal Affairs, it’s been on my list forever, I love Tony Leung and I’m sure it’s great.

But every time someone talks about how it’s self-evidently better than The Departed (which I hear a lot) they describe The Departed like it’s *Drive Angry 3-D* or something. Costigan and Sullivan are a “clear-cut hero and villain”? They don‘t “each have their own flaws and morals”? Is all that you remember from this movie a supercut of characters getting shot in the head?

If there’s one thing that The Departed isn’t “subtle” about, it’s that this isn’t a “good guys good, bad guys bad” movie. Costigan is generally a bit more sympathetic because he’s more openly vulnerable and Damon plays Sullivan as, basically, a much more effective liar. But the movie could not do more to hit you over the head with the fact that they’re not “the good guy” and “the bad guy,” they’re both sad desperate kids trapped in an amoral labyrinth and seeking validation from whatever authority figures are to hand. The big opening monologue literally asks the question “what’s the difference?” 

(I do think the movie eventually finds the difference, but it’s a typically Scorsesean, spiritual, slippery Catholic-ish answer. Basically you have to look at the way the “cops” (who are also criminals) and the “criminals“ (who are, very explicitly, also cops) treat their dead.)
  - I know what you’re saying but the reason people say that is that infernal affairs is actually much more subtle and captures the mood/themes better than the departed. What the departed is to the average American film when it comes to capturing complexity and themes, infernal affairs is to the departed. So when you see people say that the departed isn’t as subtle/complex they are comparing it to infernal affairs, they aren’t saying that the departed isn’t complex or subtle. You might disagree which is fine but if you get a chance to watch it I think you’ll see that most people have a case when they say that. 
    - I was quoting OP right above when they said “*The Departed* established a clear cut hero and villain with Billy Costigan and Colin Sullivan.” That statement isn’t dependent on anything in the other movie, and isn’t true.

I know I don’t have full context for this conversation and am talking out my ass a bit, but I feel like I’m on pretty solid ground when I say that OP is presenting a lazy caricature of a movie I dearly love out of, basically, partisanship.
3. I prefer it. It's both sleeker than The Departed while also being more philosophical and as you say, morally ambiguous. Damon played a fantastic villain but I was far more invested in Lau's character, he just felt like a more rounded human being. I still enjoyed The Departed but it took far longer to tell the same story, but all I really remember from the extra runtime was creative swearing.
4. I’ll keep reminding people and shouting from the rooftops…Infernal Affairs is better in almost every way.

Departed has tighter direction and editing, as you would expect but the script, lead performances, pacing are all better in Infernal Affairs and the plot is just executed better.
5. I prefer Infernal Affairs over The Departed, but I remember preferring Departed over the Infernal Affairs trilogy as a whole. Part 2 was great, but 3 was a letdown and I think >!the comeuppance of the mole was handled better in The Departed!<. Or should I do a rewatch?

## 2. Bugonia (2025) - we are violent apes [spoilers]

**Score:** 13
**Body:** What a beautifully shot film, with super saturated crispness that is garishly REAL, even as super real as the conspiratorial mind that animates its plot. I sat with this film for a while finally having watched it tonight, and it wasn't until I considered that the entire "twist" of the film, >!that she really IS an alien of great royalty, was instead a Wizard Of Oz *conk-head-halucination*, that I feel I started really getting it. What bothered me about the ending was that the Earth itself was portrayed as flat, as part of an ultimate reveal of what really is the case. Why would this be so? Is this just a final send up of the layers of conspiracy that envelop our thinking and politics? For me, there really was no good reason for Teddy Gatz's head to knock our leading lady out, other than to at least open up this Wizard of Oz possibility. What I'm left with is actually the brute reality that was found in Michelle Fuller's confession/reveal speech, easy to miss because it was surrounded with so much mythology and such a "pulling back the curtain" detail. Strip it all away and this is what we get: Humans just are brute and violent apes, and so much of their imagination is filled with the aggression of that ancestry, even Plemons character, played with so much polite sympathy is a horrendous, torturing, psychotic serial killer. Gatz has no really issues with his sexual abuse as a child, and politely defers, until his skull-smashing self pulverizes the confessionist (as if one of the hominid apes with a bone in 2001: A Space Odyssey). The myths and conspiracies in this sense don't really matter. If Fuller is (possibly) hallucinating that she is INDEED an alien Queen, then really the tooth-and-claw reality of the human species is ultimately the driver of it all. She is the winner, Gatz is the loser. The class politics even become erased as both conduct their yoga workouts in parallel in the morning.!<

There are many ways to meaningfully read this film, in fact it purposely opens itself up to lines of interpretation as if our desire to interpret reality itself, and "discuss" it, is part of the plague of human existence (Gatz suggests this). The problem is that what lies beneath interpretation and discussion is mythos fantasy, unbreakable delusions of explanation, pictures of the world and its past, conspiratorial explanations of outside powers variously pulling strings, whether they be advanced aliens (malicious or benevolent) or a God and the Devil...and, beneath even these sense-making powers, is the brute reality that we are violent apes. We are caught between our aggression...and the discussion of "reasons", living in various ideological pictures which operate mythologically. In the end, we are free to choose which "levels" of the film are the realist, real ones, which is the same in life. This is why the Earth was "really" flat in the end. There is no place to comfortably stand.

We are left with on the[ ritual of the Bugonia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bugonia), a "factually" wrong, but mythologically real belief that out of the rotting corpse of strength sweet honey bees will be born.

It's quite a film with very high level performances, will keep me thinking for a long time. 

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Don is a sweetheart.
Gatz is actually brilliant.
Fuller is actually bloodthirsty and vindictive.

The point of the movie is that our CEO overlords suppose themselves to be superior to the rest of human life, really to the point of believing they are superhuman, but their only real difference is the position of superiority.
2. Tell me if I'm wrong, but the autisticnkid killed himself because he thought that is how you get transported to the mither ship, right?

It's like the Heavens Gate cult in the 90s where they all killed themselves because they thought they would be transported to a ufo behind Haleys comet. 

Right?


  - I think the point is they all do something terrible even though the reasoning behind those actions are sound. In other words, being morally *right* doesn’t necessarily make you *right*. 

## 3. What films from traditional Japanese Cinema should I watch before 'MIllenium Actress' (2001)

**Score:** 4
**Body:** Howdy!

I've been working my way through Satoshi Kon's catalog. So far I've finished *Perfect Blue*, *Paranoia Agent,* and *Paprika.* I've loved all of them so far. 

I understand *Millenium Acress* to be following the life of a Japanese Actress, as well as referencing themes of Japanese Cinema. I try not to research too much into movies I watch (spoiler reasons), but that doesnt mean I like go in completely blind. 

I dont think ive seen anything from Japan pre-2000 that wasnt animated, so I figured now would be a good time to widen my horizons.

If you have film reccomendations that would help me enjoy the cultural context of *Millenium Actress* more without spoiling anything, let me know! Feel free to add extra info you might think is useful.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. the character of Chiyoko is partly based on Hara Setsuko - so definitely see her in "Tokyo Story" (1953) and "Late Spring" (1949)

Kurosawa's samurai movies gets referenced - "Throne of Blood" (1957), "The Hidden Fortress" (1958), and "Yojimbo" (1961)

there's also a kaiju flick in there so look up the OG "Godzilla" (1954)

  
Fellini's "8 1/2" (1963) makes for a good double bill with "Millennium Actress"
  - Postwar directors, Kurosawa in particular, were influenced by Russian literature. Something else to add to the mix!
  - I would argue that Late Spring and particularly Tokyo Story are essentially required. It’s a great movie either way but there are key scenes in the movie that carry a hidden weight if you’ve seen them.

>!A number of pivotal events happen on what is meant to be understood by the audience as the set of Tokyo Story.
!<

2. > dont think ive seen anything from Japan pre-2000 that wasnt animated

Well, good thing is you've got like 1500 amazing movies to discover.

I don't think Millennium Actress demands any prerequisite viewings per se, but as u/itchy_008 noted, the main character is believed to be inspired by the legendary Setsuko Hara. She was in a ton of Yasujiro Ozu and Mikio Naruse films from the 40s until her early retirement in the 60s.

Beyond that, there are endless masterpieces from Japanese cinema. I'll give you a bunch of directors worth seeking out:

Ozu

Kurosawa

Naruse

Hamaguchi

Kobayashi

Mizoguchi

Itami

Koreeda

Kitano

Iwai

Sono

K. Kurosawa

Suzuki

Imamura

Kinoshita

Shinoda

Teshigahara

Oshima

Obayashi

Anno

Miike

Somai

Ichikawa

## 4. Foggy Notion (1995)

**Score:** 4
**Body:** I am looking for a British short film from 1995 entitled [Foggy Notion](http://imdb.com/title/tt2608702/). It felt like a mix of Clerks and Reality Bites, but it was done as a documentary with improvisational documentary. The film gained more notoriety years later due to one of its stars - Geri Halliwell - becoming a popular figure due to her association with the Spice Girls.

Does a copy of the full 25-minute film exist online? I can only find short clips of it on YouTube.
